sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah, I mean, we were seeing dead people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I did not mention when we were waiting to be transported to the [?
Taiga ?] camp.
I mean, the scene was unbelievable how many dead people were laying there from the conditions.
Just some people just were not used to it.
They was just dying out.
But again, in, under those circumstances, naturally you look.
And, and you pity the family.
But everybody looked out for themself.
So-- and our aim was how fast can we escape from this place?
Anyway, when we arrived, when we were then in, in [INAUDIBLE], which is when we built our own house, which we were happy.
We had a home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was not giving to us.
We had to get our own food, which I remember, used to get up 2:00, 3:00 in the morning to go in line and wait till the store opens up and purchase whatever we can purchase.
Many times, I remember I froze my fingers.
They were frozen, which I didn't realize it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but my older sisters came.
She notice it.
She used to rub it with snow, which helped.
And one day I was walking towards the supermarket.
I used to-- I decided to take a shortcut.
And I fell in.
Naturally, the snow was very deep.
And I fell into a hole, big hole, almost three-quarter of my body.
And I was trying to get out, which I succeeded.
But I damaged my left foot, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I suffer for many, many years.
I used to work.
And I used to trip on something.
Used to be very painful.
But I-- it bothered me till 1950, when I came to United States and they cured it.
But I remember was-- this was our contribution to the family, go stay in line, you know, for foo, and go to the forest and cut logs to make a fire in the house to keep warm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Never went to school there and tried to be as helpful as I could.
Yeah.
Everybody has a part [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and did it.
But again, we did not have adequate, you know, clothing.
My older sister used to know how to, to sew, you know, on a sewing machine.
She took blankets.
And she make boots out of blankets and really saved a lot.
You didn't freeze in your legs.
And whatever anybody could participate in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] good memories, we did not have there.
Because one day, they arrested my father and accused him being a counter revolutionary in the camp, saying that he had seen Russian youth, Communist youth, singing songs.
And he went over and he hit them, told them to stop, otherwise he's going to hit them.
This was his accusation.
Naturally, arrested him.
And they took him to Sverdlovsk.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was indicted to be executed.
But again, the whole family got together.
And we thought, what can we do to help him?
Naturally, my older sister and then my older brothers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to Sverdlovsk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we bribed the commander of the camp.
We were there.
And he made the arrangements we should go to Sverdlovsk.
Same time, we got a connection of the head of the jail and the judge and big officials.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were lucky that we managed to take out from Poland a lot of valuable things.
Like the women gave their diamonds, their gold watches.
We had pieces of gold.
We had money, Prussian money.
And that's how we succeeded to keep him, you know, not being executed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then there were rumors that the Polish army and the Russian are trying to make peace between themself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just rumors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Russian had announced amnesty to the prisoners, Polish prisoners.
And my father was one of them.
He came out from jail.
He didn't know anybody.
He didn't know how to go back.
But he was walking.
And he came across a Jew, a Russian Jew, because he didn't know any Russian.
And in, in Jewish, he communicated with that Jew.
And the Jew had-- gave him direction.
One night, like 2 o'clock in the morning, he was afraid to wake up the family.
And he knocked the window where, where the men slept.
Naturally, we all woke up.
And this was him-- you know, a big celebration and crying and everything else.
And, and they liberated us, the Russians.
They said, now you're free.
You can move around.
Not any more prisoners, because you are friends of ours.
They were considering us as Poles.
And then we decided to move Uzbekistan.
It was a warm climate.
And the main reason was that it's a concentration of the Polish army was in that area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we moved there, again, on our own.
And we moved to a town.
The name was Ziadin.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We rented a house.
And we lived there.
My--one of my brother-in-laws got a job in the headquarters of the Polish agency.
And it was really all the difference in the world.
We were free.
We were able to shop and get back to a little more normal life.
And again, I-- we didn't go to school.
Because we felt it's temporarily and thinking what is our next step.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because of our brother-in-law who worked the headquarters, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he found out that orphans, Polish orphans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have to register and will leave Russia, to where, nobody knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My father decided-- no, this was only Polish, not Jews-- underline, Polish orphans.
My father decided to give me and my younger brother a Polish first name.
Because second name didn't make a different, you know?
Feit, they didn't know it's Polish or Jew.
And we manage to join the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the first camp, which started to be organized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some way or another, some kids pointed out on us that we are Jews.
And they told us we cannot go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this was ongoing thing.
And we joined a second run, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we succeeded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brought us to a port by trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of my brothers and sisters went after us, which was my oldest sister, Chana, my nephew, [INAUDIBLE], Miriam, Joe, and Isadore.
And they took a chance.
They thought maybe they'll be able to join us.
They saw us going on the ship.
It was my younger brother, myself, and my oldest sister as a supervisor, and my nephew.
They tried.
But they were caught.
One sister was put in jail.
And my other-- the rest of the family managed to escape.
And they remained in Russia.
He felt it's the first opportunity that some of the family should go abroad.
Because they knew it, sooner or later, they will join to leave Russia.
But he felt quicker is better.
Is-- listen, we, we were about 1,200 children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't realize what happening-- nice boat, English boat, supervised, taking care of us beautifully.
And they were saying that we are going to a Polish camp in Tehran, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Iran, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Naturally, it took about eight months till we got there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first step that we ended was in India, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is now-- it was the, the city Karachi, which is now Pakistan.
They took us to the Polish camps.
And we stayed there under the Polish army supervision for many months.
Just nothing-- slept in the tent.
And a few times, they took as with buses to see this, you know, the town and the zoo, took us around.
But it was in a sanded area where military camps normally are put up.
And we were there.
We were like, you know, overdosed.
We didn't know what's going on, separated.
There were children different ages, I would say from two years and up, up to 15, 16-year-old kids.
The bigger kids used to take care of the littler ones.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was not a happy time.
I mean, I cannot say good memories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they decided to take us to Tehran.
In Tehran, they put us the outskirt of Tehran, again, in a Polish camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we heard that the Jewish agency is in Tehran and trying to get us to go to Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
There was a big conflicts for many months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the foreign minister, Moshe Sharett, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and his wife were there.
There was a Israeli, not Israeli, Jewish embassy, you know?
And they were the one who were fighting for us.
There was also the Joint from America, which they were trying to get us and bring us to America.
Naturally, they didn't succeed.
And one day, the announcement was that we're going to Israel.
One day before that was that we're going to Australia with the Polish army, but didn't materialize.
But they put us again on a big English ship, military ship.
We were 1,200 children plus personnel.
Loaded us up, and we were heading towards Israel under tremendous escort.
We were in the middle.
There were 10 military destroyers, all kind of different military ships watching us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] being on the ship, we heard that one of the ships was hit by a Italian torpedo and was sank.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some way or another, the news got to Israel.
The news was that our ship was hit.
And we were drowned.
There were headlines in Israel by the morning, you know, what happening.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we, when we arrived, we arrived to Alexandria, Egypt.
And they put us on train.
And they transferred us to Israel.
And arriving in Israel was a tremendous celebration.
Because you know, we are, we come again and a few days before, that we were drowned.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- in Israel when we arrived, they took us to a, a center, a school all the kids.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then again, different organizations were trying to get as many children to their side.
But supposedly was up to the children.
And I had relatives, and many had, that came to visit us.
And we used to discuss it.
But anyway, I selected to go to Mikveh Israel, which was one of the oldest agriculture schools in Israel, which I made a good choice.
The other choices were to go to [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to [?
Mizrahi ?] [INAUDIBLE] Israel.
Any organization had the right into your children and try to influence, influence them to join them with promises, you know?
The big difference was that if you went to [INAUDIBLE], you went to [INAUDIBLE] kibbutz, which normally they had their own school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not religious, but I was, you know, religious-oriented.
And I figure, I had my background.
I would like to go to a, a religious school, which was Mikveh Israel.
Some went to more orthodox, which I was very happy.
And I feel that that school gave me my base education in my life, to go out in the free world and manage-- excellent education, excellent supervision.
Being at that school, I, I joined the Haganah.
We-- general education, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of agriculture, which the purpose of that school was to become farmer, to join a kibbutz, moshav, moshavim.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did specialize in agriculture equipment, mechanical equipment.
And also, in that school, I worked in a, in the mechanical field, which the head of the mechanical field was a person who invented the Davidka, if you know what it is.
This was a weapons who saved Israel, which the Arabs thought, this is the atom bomb that Israel has.
And I was a part of it, helping build, welding, fixing, fitting, and inventing myself certain things.
Like you were not allowed to use live ammunition.
But you had to teach exercise and teach the soldiers when you put the trigger, when you aim, where does it end up?
And this you learned in Mikveh Israel?
Yeah.
Yeah.
There was-- we had, you know, the underground.
We have slicks.
We used to hide ammunition.
Actually, it was the, I would say, the main quarters of the underground for Tel Aviv.
We used to have the hidden places for ammunition, for weapons.
And we used to train there.
And we used to also accept new members to the Haganah in Mikveh.
No, this was 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three, four, and the beginning of five.
No.
I was in Mikveh till, I would say, the middle of 1945.
Very active in the Haganah.
I joined the Haganah in 1943.
They were enlisting.
And I joined it immediately.
It was also time they were enlisting people to the Jewish Brigade during the Second World War, which were with the English army, the Jewish brigade, which I didn't.
I was too young.
They didn't let me.
But I did join the Haganah which was, I was very active.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sent over-- was sent out to a military course, which I finished, became a corporal, in charge 12 people.
